text: The growth of lithium microstructures during cycling remains a major obstacle towards the successful commercialization of Li-metal batteries with high energy density. Using in situ 7Li MRI, in this study we elucidate the correlation between the dynamics of the electrolyte composition and the morphological characteristics of lithium formed in Li symmetric cells, by employing CSI to differentiate between various environments (deposited lithium at different time, etc. based on their distinct bulk magnetic susceptibility); namely between the mossy lithium deposits and the undesirable and detrimental runoff dendrites. It is demonstrated for the first time that in all investigated current densities mossy lithium structures start to form within a few seconds upon charging; while their transition into the undesirable runoff dendrites occur at a specific onset time, occurring at the Sand’s time at high current densities; while at low current densities the onset time of such dendrites’ formation occurs much earlier than the time estimated from the mass transport considerations, indicating the effect of other less dominant sources at low current densities such as: a) the surface inhomogeneities on the electrolyte/ electrode surface, b) instability in the SEI layer; thus bridging the gap towards understanding various sources of lithium microstructure formations, enabling a robust experimental understanding of their formations.
